# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-construction details, task-definition details, dataset-size and codebase-scale metrics, baseline model results, retrieval/context findings, fine-tuning dataset details, and stated limitations for SWE-bench.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench contains 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“We introduce SWE-bench, an evaluation framework consisting of 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories.”

## Note

The source frames these as real-world software engineering problems sourced from issue/PR pairs.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In SWE-bench, a model receives an issue text description and a complete codebase, and must generate a patch that resolves the issue.

## Source Excerpt

“Model input. A model is given an issue text description and a complete codebase. The model is then tasked to make an edit to the codebase to resolve the issue. In practice, we represent edits as patch files...”

## Note

This evidence describes the benchmark task formulation, not model performance.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A proposed solution counts as resolved only if the generated patch applies successfully and all associated unit and system tests pass.

## Source Excerpt

“If the patch applies successfully and all of these tests pass we consider the proposed solution to have successfully resolved the issue. The metric for our benchmark is the percentage of task instances that are resolved.”

## Note

The benchmark metric is percentage of task instances resolved under this execution-based criterion.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Under the paper’s evaluations, the best-performing model, Claude 2, resolved 1.96% of SWE-bench issues.

## Source Excerpt

“The best-performing model, Claude 2, is able to solve a mere 1.96% of the issues.”

## Note

The abstract presents this as the headline benchmark result.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The benchmark-construction pipeline started from about 90,000 pull requests collected from 12 popular open-source Python repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“We start by collecting pull requests (PRs) from 12 popular open-source Python repositories on GitHub, producing about ∼90,000 PRs in total.”

## Note

This is the initial scale before filtering.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Attribute-based filtering kept merged pull requests that both resolved a GitHub issue and changed repository test files.

## Source Excerpt

“We create candidate tasks by selecting the merged PRs that (1) resolve a GitHub issue and (2) make changes to the test files of the repository...”

## Note

The test-file criterion is used as an indicator that the contributor added tests for the issue.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: method
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Execution-based filtering required at least one test to change from fail to pass after applying the pull request, and removed instances with installation or runtime errors.

## Source Excerpt

“We filter out task instances without at least one test where its status changes from a fail to pass... We also filter out instances that result in installation or runtime errors.”

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it specifies a filtering condition for what counts as a usable instance rather than how a model solves the task. It is contradiction-relevant because it preserves a limitation on which real-world issues remain in the benchmark.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
After filtering, the original roughly 90,000 pull requests were reduced to 2,294 task instances.

## Source Excerpt

“Through these stages of filtering, the original 90,000 PRs are filtered down to the 2,294 task instances which comprise SWE-bench.”

## Note

This evidence gives the final benchmark size after the stated filtering pipeline.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.3 and Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Issue descriptions average 195 words, and benchmark codebases average 3,010 non-test files and 438K non-test lines.

## Source Excerpt

“Issue descriptions are typically long and detailed (195 words on average), and codebases regularly contain many thousands of files.” Table 1 reports “Issue Text Length (Words) 195.1,” “Codebase # Files (non-test) 3,010,” and “# Lines (non-test) 438K.”

## Note

These are average task-instance scale statistics from the benchmark description.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Reference solutions in SWE-bench average edits to 1.7 files, 3.0 functions, and 32.8 added-or-removed lines.

## Source Excerpt

“SWE-bench’s reference solutions average editing 1.7 files, 3.0 functions, and 32.8 lines (added or removed).”

## Note

This evidence describes the average size of gold solutions, not generated model patches.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each task has at least one fail-to-pass test, 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests, and the median instance runs 51 additional tests.

## Source Excerpt

“For each task instance, there is at least one fail-to-pass test... and 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests... In addition, a median of 51 additional tests run to check whether prior functionality is properly maintained.”

## Note

This evidence describes the evaluation-test structure for benchmark instances.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 2.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench Lite is a 300-instance subset sampled to be more self-contained and focused on functional bug fixes, and it covers 11 of the original 12 repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“To encourage adoption of SWE-bench, we create a Lite subset of 300 instances from SWE-bench that have been sampled to be more self-contained, with a focus on evaluating functional bug fixes... SWE-bench Lite covers 11 of the original 12 repositories...”

## Note

This evidence concerns the paper’s smaller evaluation subset rather than the main benchmark.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The training dataset used for SWE-Llama contains 19,000 issue–PR pairs from 37 additional popular Python repositories, and those repositories are disjoint from the evaluation benchmark repositories.

## Source Excerpt

“We follow our data collection procedure and collect 19,000 issue-PR pairs from an additional 37 popular Python package repositories... To eliminate the risk of data contamination, the set of repositories in the training data is disjoint from those included in the evaluation benchmark.”

## Note

This evidence describes the fine-tuning data source and the contamination-control measure.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: method
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
For memory efficiency, the authors fine-tuned only attention-sublayer weights with LoRA and excluded training sequences longer than 30,000 tokens, reducing the effective training corpus to 10,000 instances.

## Source Excerpt

“For memory efficiency, we fine-tune only the weights of the attention sublayer using LoRA... and exclude training sequences with more than 30,000 tokens, reducing the effective size of the training corpus to 10,000 instances.”

## Note

This is mechanism-bounded because it specifies a training constraint rather than a direct causal account of benchmark performance. It is contradiction-relevant because it attaches a material limitation to the reported fine-tuning setup.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 4.1 and Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
With BM25 retrieval, Claude 2 performed worse as maximum context increased, dropping from 1.96% resolved at 13k context to 1.22% at 50k context.

## Source Excerpt

Table 2 reports for Claude 2: “13k 1.96,” “27k 1.87,” and “50k 1.22.” The text adds: “From observation, models perform best on the shortest context window...”

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it shows a performance pattern consistent with context-localization limits without fully establishing the cause. It is contradiction-relevant because more retrieved context improved recall but coincided with lower task resolution.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 5 and Table 6
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Claude 2 resolved 4.8% of issues with oracle retrieval and 5.93% with oracle-collapsed retrieval, versus 1.96% with BM25 retrieval at 13k context.

## Source Excerpt

“There, Claude 2 is able to resolve 4.8% of issues using the ‘oracle’ retriever.” Table 6 reports for Claude 2 under “Oracle”-collapsed: “Resolved 5.93.” Table 2 reports BM25 at 13k: “Claude 2 1.96.”

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it reflects sensitivity to retrieval/context setup rather than a full explanation of model behavior. It is contradiction-relevant because performance changes materially across evaluation settings.
---

In SWE-bench, a model receives an issue text description and a complete codebase, and must generate a patch that resolves the issue.

## Source Excerpt

“Model input. A model is given an issue text description and a complete codebase. The model is then tasked to make an edit to the codebase to resolve the issue. In practice, we represent edits as patch files...”

## Note

This evidence describes the benchmark task formulation, not model performance.
```

---

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 4.1 and Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
With BM25 retrieval, Claude 2 performed worse as maximum context increased, dropping from 1.96% resolved at 13k context to 1.22% at 50k context.

## Source Excerpt

Table 2 reports for Claude 2: “13k 1.96,” “27k 1.87,” and “50k 1.22.” The text adds: “From observation, models perform best on the shortest context window...”

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it shows a performance pattern consistent with context-localization limits without fully establishing the cause. It is contradiction-relevant because more retrieved context improved recall but coincided with lower task resolution.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Jimenez et al., 2024, Section 5 and Table 6
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Claude 2 resolved 4.8% of issues with oracle retrieval and 5.93% with oracle-collapsed retrieval, versus 1.96% with BM25 retrieval at 13k context.

## Source Excerpt

“There, Claude 2 is able to resolve 4.8% of issues using the ‘oracle’ retriever.” Table 6 reports for Claude 2 under “Oracle”-collapsed: “Resolved 5.93.” Table 2 reports BM25 at 13k: “Claude 2 1.96.”

## Note

This evidence is mechanism-bounded because it reflects sensitivity to retrieval/context setup rather than a full explanation of model behavior. It is contradiction-relevant because performance changes materially across evaluation settings.
